notable_findings: Highest risk cell at (9.00, 118.00) with 89% probability — southern Palawan near mining concessions
6.8% of analyzed cells (156 of 2,300) are high-risk, concentrated along the western coastal corridor
Frontier distance is the dominant predictor (42.8%) — deforestation expands outward from existing cleared areas
recommendations: Strengthen enforcement of EO 23 (nationwide logging moratorium) in southern Palawan municipalities where loss rates exceed 4%/year, particularly in areas adjacent to mining operations under DENR Administrative Order 2010-21.

Expand NIPAS Act coverage to high-risk forested areas not currently within the Puerto Princesa Subterranean River National Park or El Nido-Taytay Managed Resource Protected Area — the 2,300-cell analysis identifies 156 cells requiring priority attention.
Deploy CENRO field monitoring teams to the 5 identified hotspot coordinates, prioritizing cells with >75% deforestation probability for quarterly ground-truth surveys.

Coordinate with PCSD (Palawan Council for Sustainable Development) to review Strategic Environmental Plan compliance for developments within 5km of deforestation frontiers, which the model identifies as the strongest risk predictor.
Establish a community-based forest monitoring program using the Guardian model — local communities report encroachment via mobile app, validated by PENRO ecologists remotely.
executive_summary: Analysis of 2,300 grid cells (0.02° resolution) across Palawan reveals that 6.8% (156 cells, approximately 7,570 hectares) are classified as high deforestation risk. The primary risk driver is proximity to existing deforestation frontiers (42.8% feature importance), followed by historical annual loss rate (36.1% importance), indicating that deforestation expands outward from existing cleared areas. The highest-risk zone is concentrated in southern Palawan near mining concessions, with the single highest-risk cell at coordinates (9.00°N, 118.00°E) showing 89% deforestation probability.
